ground_truth.title: Plumbers are training as substitute teachers so full time teachers can protest a bill
ground_truth.author: Sergio Martínez-Beltrán | The Texas Newsroom
ground_truth.main_content: Texas plumbers are getting trained as substitute teachers to allow teachers to protest at the state Capitol against a school vouchers bill. The Texas Legislature is holding a special session this week, and a main issue is school vouchers. A bill, if passed, would allow parents to use taxpayer dollars to help pay for private, parochial or home-schooling for their children. At least 30 states have similar voucher-like programs. But Texas public school teachers are pushing back hard, and they're getting help from an unlikely group - union plumbers. Travis Cantwell, a plumber and an HVAC technician, along with a handful of other plumbers and HVAC workers, is training to become a substitute teacher so full-time educators can take the day off to fight the vouchers bill. Thomas Kennedy, the executive director of the Texas State Building Trades, a coalition of union construction workers, plumbers, pipefitters and electricians, has finished his two-hour training to become a substitute teacher in the Austin Independent School District. Republicans in the Texas legislature are pushing hard for the proposal, saying it would give students in low-income communities an opportunity to have a better education. Zeph Capo, the president of the Texas American Federation of Teachers, says what plumbers are doing will help, and hopes the new coalition of teachers and construction workers will defeat school vouchers in Texas once again.
with: Texas plumbers are getting trained as substitute teachers to allow teachers to protest at the state Capitol against a school vouchers bill.
A bill, if passed, would allow parents to use taxpayer dollars to help pay for private, parochial or home-schooling for their children.
Cantwell says he and his fellow union plumbers want to do all they can to help teachers fight the vouchers bill.
The idea of getting plumbers involved came in part from the American Federation of Teachers and Thomas Kennedy, the executive director of the Texas State Building Trades.
Republicans in the Texas legislature are pushing hard for the proposal, saying it would give students in low-income communities an opportunity to have a better education.
